verses.flow: But now your kingdom shall not endure. The LORD has sought out for Himself a man after His own heart, and the LORD has appointed him as ruler over His people, because you have not kept what the LORD commanded you."
verses.note: HEBREW: "ish kilbavo" — "a man after His own heart." The word lev (heart) in Hebrew denotes the will, purpose, and decision-making center — not emotion. A man after God's heart is one whose will aligns with God's will. This is the first prophecy of David, though unnamed. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: David as the man after God's heart is the type; Christ is the antitype — the one whose will perfectly aligned with the Father's (John 6:38). PROPHETIC: This verse announces the entire Davidic covenant trajectory.

verses.flow: And Samuel said, "Has the LORD as great a delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as in obeying the voice of the LORD? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice, and to listen than the fat of rams.
verses.note: HEBREW: The poetic oracle of vv.22-23 is one of the most theologically dense passages in the Old Testament. "shama'" (to hear/obey) is set against "zevach" (sacrifice). Obedience is not merely better than sacrifice — it IS the sacrifice God desires. "Rebellion (meri) is as the sin of divination (qesem)" — disobedience and sorcery are equated because both attempt to manipulate reality apart from God's word. verses.flow: So Samuel said to him, "The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to your neighbor, who is better than you.
And also the Glory of Israel will not lie or change His mind; for He is not a man that He should change His mind."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Netsach Yisra'el" — "the Glory/Eminence/Enduring One of Israel." This is a unique divine title, occurring only here. netsach can mean "glory," "eminence," "perpetuity," or "victory." The declaration "will not lie or change His mind" (lo yeshaqer velo yinnachem) seems to contradict v.11 ("I regret"), creating a deliberate theological tension. THEOLOGICAL: The resolution is that God's eternal purposes are immutable (v.29), while His relational responses to human actions are genuine (v.11). God's character does not change; His dealings with humans respond to their choices.

verses.flow: Then a champion came out from the armies of the Philistines named Goliath, from Gath, whose height was six cubits and a span.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Champion" ('ish habbenayim) — literally "the man of the between," i.e., the one who stands in the space between the two armies for representative combat. Goliath's height "six cubits and a span" (~9 feet 9 inches in the MT; the LXX reads "four cubits and a span" ~6 feet 9 inches). The MT preserves the older tradition of a superhuman warrior echoing the Nephilim/Rephaim traditions (cf. Num. 13:33; Deut. 2:10-11; 2 Sam. 21:16-22).

verses.flow: And he had a bronze helmet on his head, and he was clothed with scale armor, and the weight of the armor was five thousand shekels of bronze.
verses.note: HEBREW: The detailed description of Goliath's armor — bronze helmet, scale armor (shiryon qasqassim), bronze greaves, bronze javelin — is unique in its specificity. The total weight of his armor ("five thousand shekels of bronze" = ~125 lbs) emphasizes the superhuman scale of the threat. The narrator catalogs every piece to show what David will face without any of it.

He then adds the staggering prayer: "May the LORD be with you as He has been with my father." Jonathan acknowledges that the LORD's presence is transferring from the house of Saul to David.

verses.flow: And if I am still alive, will you not show me the lovingkindness of the LORD, that I may not die?
verses.note: HEBREW: "The lovingkindness of the LORD" (chesed YHWH) — Jonathan asks David for divine-quality loyalty. The phrase chesed YHWH appears only here and in 2 Sam. 9:3 (where David fulfills this request for Mephibosheth). Jonathan foresees his own death and asks that God's covenantal love extend through David to his descendants. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: David's later fulfillment of this covenant with Mephibosheth (2 Sam. 9) — seeking out the crippled heir of a dead king to seat him at the royal table — is one of the most powerful pictures of grace in the Old Testament, and a direct type of Christ seeking the unworthy to share His table.

verses.flow: So he said to his men, "Far be it from me because of the LORD that I should do this thing to my lord, the LORD's anointed, to stretch out my hand against him, since he is the LORD's anointed."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The LORD's anointed" (meshiach YHWH) — this phrase is the theological key to David's restraint. Mashiach (anointed one) carries sacral meaning: the one upon whom God has placed His hand. David will not take the kingdom by violence against God's existing anointed, even though God has designated David as the next anointed. He trusts God's timing and God's method. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: David's refusal to seize by force what God has promised him by right is a type of Christ's refusal of Satan's shortcut to the kingdoms of the world (Matt. 4:8-10). Both David and Jesus wait for God's appointed time rather than grasping power prematurely.

verses.flow: But David said to Abishai, "Do not destroy him, for who can stretch out his hand against the LORD's anointed and be without guilt?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Who can stretch out his hand against the LORD's anointed and be without guilt?" (mi shalach yado bimeshiach YHWH weniqqa) — David articulates the principle a second time. This repetition (cf. 24:6) is not redundancy but theological reinforcement. David's restraint is not situational but principled. Even when Abishai offers to do the killing for him, David refuses to permit it. The sanctity of God's anointed is inviolable regardless of that anointed one's behavior.

verses.flow: David also said, "As the LORD lives, surely the LORD will strike him, or his day will come that he dies, or he will go down into battle and perish.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: David offers three alternatives to killing Saul: "the LORD will strike him, or his day will come that he dies, or he will go down into battle and perish." All three remove the matter from human hands. David envisions natural death, judicial death, or military death — all under God's sovereignty. The third option is prophetic: Saul will indeed "go down into battle and perish" (ch. 31).

verses.flow: Now therefore, please let my lord the king listen to the words of his servant. If the LORD has stirred you up against me, let Him accept an offering; but if it is men, cursed are they before the LORD, for they have driven me out today so that I would have no share in the inheritance of the LORD, saying, 'Go, serve other gods.'
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: David's theological argument is profound: if the LORD has stirred Saul against him, an offering can resolve it. But if men are responsible, they are cursed for driving David away from "the inheritance of the LORD" (nachalat YHWH) — the promised land where YHWH is worshiped. To be exiled from the land is to be told "Go, serve other gods" — forced into a context where YHWH's worship is absent. Land, worship, and divine presence are inseparable in Israelite theology.

verses.flow: Then David said in his heart, "Now I will perish one day by the hand of Saul. There is nothing better for me than to escape into the land of the Philistines. Saul then will despair of searching for me anymore in all the territory of Israel, and I will escape from his hand."

This stands in tension with his earlier confidence that God would protect him (17:37; 23:14). The narrator reports David's reasoning without endorsing it. Even the anointed can falter in faith. God's faithfulness does not depend on David's consistency.

verses.flow: Now Samuel was dead, and all Israel had lamented him and buried him in Ramah, his own city. And Saul had removed the mediums and the spiritists from the land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The narrator reminds us of two facts: Samuel is dead, and Saul expelled the mediums. Both facts matter for what follows. Samuel's death means the prophetic voice is silent. The expulsion of mediums means Saul himself criminalized the very practice he is about to seek. Every element of this chapter is constructed to show the completeness of Saul's spiritual collapse.

verses.flow: When Saul inquired of the LORD, the LORD did not answer him, either by dreams or by Urim or by prophets.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The LORD did not answer him, either by dreams or by Urim or by prophets" — the three channels of divine communication are all closed. Dreams (personal revelation), Urim (priestly mediation via the ephod), and prophets (Samuel is dead, and Saul has no prophetic voice). God's silence is itself a form of judgment. Saul has systematically destroyed his access to each channel: he alienated David who had the prophetic anointing, he massacred the priests at Nob who held the Urim, and Samuel is dead.

verses.flow: Then Saul said to his servants, "Seek for me a woman who is a medium, that I may go to her and inquire of her." And his servants said to him, "Behold, there is a woman who is a medium at En-dor."
verses.note: HEBREW: "A woman who is a medium" ('eshet ba'alat 'ov) — literally "a woman, a mistress of a necromantic spirit." The 'ov is the spirit of the dead or the ritual pit used to contact them. Saul had previously expelled all mediums (v.3) in obedience to Deut. 18:10-12. His turn to necromancy represents the final inversion of his kingship: the one who was supposed to enforce God's law now violates its most fundamental prohibition.

verses.flow: And the king said to her, "Do not be afraid; but what do you see?" And the woman said to Saul, "I see a divine being coming up out of the earth."
verses.note: HEBREW: "I see a divine being coming up out of the earth" ('elohim ra'iti 'olim min ha'arets) — 'elohim here is debated. It can mean "God," "gods," "divine beings," or "a spirit." The woman sees a supernatural figure rising from Sheol. The plural verb ('olim, "coming up") with singular referent is unusual. THEOLOGICAL: This passage raises profound questions about the afterlife, the divine council, and whether God actually permitted Samuel's spirit to return. The text presents it as genuinely Samuel (v.15-19, his prophecy comes true), suggesting God sovereignly permitted this encounter as Saul's final judgment oracle.

Had David fought alongside the Philistines against Israel, he would have been fighting against his own people and potentially against Jonathan. verses.flow: Moreover David was greatly distressed because the people spoke of stoning him, for all the people were embittered, each one because of his sons and his daughters. But David strengthened himself in the LORD his God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "David strengthened himself in the LORD his God" (wayyitchazeq David baYHWH 'Elohav) — this is the turning point. At his lowest moment — his city burned, his families captured, his own men talking of stoning him — David turns not to strategy but to God. The verb hitchazeq (reflexive of chazaq) means to make oneself strong, to find renewed strength. David does this "in the LORD" — his strength comes from outside himself. This stands in direct contrast to Saul, who at his lowest moment turns to a medium (ch. 28). The two kings' responses to crisis reveal the fundamental difference between them.

verses.flow: Then Saul said to his armor bearer, "Draw your sword and thrust me through with it, lest these uncircumcised come and thrust me through and abuse me." But his armor bearer would not, for he was greatly afraid. So Saul took his sword and fell on it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Saul's suicide — "he took his sword and fell on it" — is the final act of a king who has lived by his own will rather than God's. His fear of being "abused" (hit'allelu) by the uncircumcised echoes David's concern about the uncircumcised Philistine in ch. 17, but with opposite outcome. David trusted God and prevailed; Saul trusted himself and perished.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Saul's weapons are placed in the temple of Ashtaroth and his body fastened to the wall of Beth-shan. The Philistines credit their gods for the victory. verses.flow: Now when the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard what the Philistines had done to Saul,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The men of Jabesh-gilead remember that Saul rescued them from Nahash the Ammonite (ch. 11) — his first and arguably greatest act as king. In his death, the only ones who show him honor are those he saved at the beginning of his reign. Loyalty endures. Their night march, recovery of the bodies, and burial under the tamarisk tree constitute the only act of chesed in Saul's death narrative.

verses.flow: all the valiant men arose and walked all night, and took the body of Saul and the bodies of his sons from the wall of Beth-shan, and they came to Jabesh and burned them there.
verses.note: HEBREW: "All the valiant men" (kol 'ish chayil) — the men of Jabesh march all night, a round trip of roughly 25 miles across hostile territory, to recover the bodies. This is military-grade courage motivated by gratitude. They "burned them there" — unusual for Israelite practice (normally burial without cremation), perhaps to prevent the Philistines from further desecrating the remains.

verses.flow: And they took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree at Jabesh, and fasted seven days.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Fasted seven days" — the full mourning period for Saul. The book of 1 Samuel ends not with David's triumph but with Israel's grief. Saul's story is a tragedy in the classical sense: a man of great potential undone by disobedience, jealousy, and the departure of God's Spirit. The narrative arc from 1 Sam. 9 (tall, handsome, chosen by God) to 1 Sam. 31 (dead on Gilboa, head cut off, body nailed to a wall) is one of the most devastating character trajectories in all of scripture. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Mount Gilboa, where God's anointed king falls, stands in contrast to Mount Calvary, where God's Anointed King falls — but rises. Saul's death is final; Christ's death is the beginning of victory.
